paper_title: Peatland Model Simulation and Carbon Metrics Calculation

methods.description: Process for setting up and running the DigiBog peatland model simulation, including defining the physical environment, boundary conditions, parameters, and applying driving climate data (baseline and perturbed).

steps.description: In 10 we simulated a 2-D transect of raised bog with a radius of 150 m (75 × 2 m × 2 m columns). Here we used the same length transect over a sloping impermeable mineral soil base (3°).
steps.searchable_summary: Define model spatial domain and base topography for a 2D peatland transect.
outputs: Initialized model spatial grid and base conditions

steps.description: A no-flow condition at the top of the slope represented a drainage divide, and a fixed water level condition at the downslope margin represented a stream.
steps.searchable_summary: Set hydrological boundary conditions for the model simulation.
outputs: Defined boundary conditions (no-flow, fixed water level)

steps.description: The model was set up as described by 40 with the parameter values shown in Table 1. The parameters fall within the range of values used for previous DigiBog simulations 33,40 . We used the version of DigiBog described by 10 .
steps.searchable_summary: Configure model parameters and select model version based on previous studies.
outputs: Configured model instance with specified parameters and version

steps.description: Our baseline simulation used the driving data from 10 .
steps.searchable_summary: Obtain baseline climate driving data for the simulation.
inputs: Reference to baseline driving data source (10)
outputs: Baseline climate driving data (time series of precipitation minus evapotranspiration, temperature)

steps.description: We modified these data with perturbations to temperature and net rainfall of 400 years duration starting 1600 years and 600 years ago respectively (as described in the main text and shown in Fig. 7).
steps.searchable_summary: Apply specified perturbations to the baseline climate driving data.
inputs: Perturbation parameters (temperature change, net rainfall change, duration: 400 years, start times: 1600 and 600 years ago)

steps.description: DigiBog simulates the accumulation of peat over millennial timescales by adding layers of plant litter to each column... Decomposition of whole or part peat layers is also dependent on air temperature and on water-table position... Water-table position is determined in a hydrological sub-model... Detailed descriptions of DigiBog's routines can be found in 40 .
steps.searchable_summary: Run the DigiBog peatland simulation with configured setup and driving data.
outputs: Simulation results (time series of peat layer properties (age, mass) per column, column height per year, water table position, etc.) over simulation timescale

methods.description: Procedures for calculating apparent carbon accumulation rate (aCAR) and net carbon balance (NCB) from the annual outputs generated by the DigiBog model simulation, using peat layer mass/age and column height data.

steps.description: We selected one of the columns from the middle our simulated peatland (column 37-i.e. its centre was 77 m from the drainage divide) and, starting at the base of this virtual core, we calculated the average mass of each pair of layers (i.e. layers 1 and 2, layers 2 and 3, etc.)
steps.searchable_summary: Select a specific model column and calculate the average mass for consecutive peat layers.
inputs: Model output data (peat layer mass per column)
outputs: Average mass for each pair of consecutive layers in the selected column

steps.description: ...and divided the result by the age difference in years between the two layers.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate peat mass accumulation rate per unit area by dividing average layer pair mass by age difference.
inputs: Age of each layer (from model output)
outputs: Peat mass accumulation rate per unit area (g m^-2 yr^-1) for each layer pair

steps.description: To estimate the rate of C accumulation, the resulting peat mass was multiplied by 0.5 17 .
steps.searchable_summary: Convert peat mass accumulation rate to apparent carbon accumulation rate (aCAR) using a carbon fraction.
inputs: Peat mass accumulation rate per unit area
outputs: Apparent carbon accumulation rate (aCAR) (g C m^-2 yr^-1)

steps.description: The model also records the height of each peat column at the end of each year.
steps.searchable_summary: Extract annual peat column height data from model output.
inputs: Model output data (column height per year)
outputs: Time series of peat column height for each column

steps.description: To calculate the change in C stock (NCB) between two consecutive years, we subtracted the height of a peat column at year t i-1 from that at year t i and converted the change in height into a change in peat mass (our peat has a bulk density of 100 kg m −3 )-that is to say, we followed Eq. (3), �S p /�t = S p,i − S p,i−1 /(t i − t i−1 ).
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate annual change in peat mass per unit area using column height change and bulk density.
inputs: Time series of peat column height (for a column)
outputs: Annual change in peat mass per unit area (g m^-2 yr^-1) time series (for a column)

steps.description: ...and again multiplied the result by 0.5 to give the change in the C store.
steps.searchable_summary: Convert annual change in peat mass to annual change in carbon stock (NCB) using a carbon fraction.
inputs: Annual change in peat mass per unit area time series
outputs: Annual change in carbon stock (NCB) (g C m^-2 yr^-1) time series (for a column)

steps.description: We compared the mean annual NCB with the Long-Term Rate of C Accumulation (LORCA) 11 (Table 2). LORCA (M L −2 T −1 ) is calculated for the whole of a peatland's developmental history (a dated layer of 6000 years in our case) and therefore accounts for all the additions and losses of C during this time... As a result, mean annual NCB and LORCA should be the same 11 and they were ( Table 2).
steps.searchable_summary: Compare the mean annual NCB with the Long-Term Rate of C Accumulation (LORCA) for validation.
inputs: Annual NCB time series (for a column)
LORCA value (from Table 2 or calculated separately)
outputs: Comparison result (e.g., mean NCB value, confirmation of agreement with LORCA)
